title: When Brazil Built Its Capital on Modernist Principles: The Controversial Design of Bras√≠lia
description: When we think of mod¬≠ern archi¬≠tec¬≠ture, we often think first of what‚Äôs called the Inter¬≠na¬≠tion¬≠al Style, whose min¬≠i¬≠mal¬≠ist, rec¬≠ti¬≠lin¬≠ea
body:
When we think of mod¬≠ern archi¬≠tec¬≠ture, we often think first of what‚Äôs called the Inter¬≠na¬≠tion¬≠al Style, whose min¬≠i¬≠mal¬≠ist, rec¬≠ti¬≠lin¬≠ear, dec¬≠o¬≠ra¬≠tion-free forms were cham¬≠pi¬≠oned by the likes of Wal¬≠ter Gropius, Lud¬≠wig Mies van der Rohe, and Le Cor¬≠busier. Though they did build projects all over the world, that isn‚Äôt exact¬≠ly the rea¬≠son for the name. In fact, the Inter¬≠na¬≠tion¬≠al Style rep¬≠re¬≠sents an attempt to devel¬≠op a cul¬≠tur¬≠al¬≠ly neu¬≠tral aes¬≠thet¬≠ic for all built envi¬≠ron¬≠ments, deploy¬≠able equal¬≠ly in Europe, Asia, the Amer¬≠i¬≠c¬≠as, and every¬≠where else besides. That pre¬≠tense to uni¬≠ver¬≠sal¬≠i¬≠ty may count as the most utopi¬≠an aspect of an avowed¬≠ly utopi¬≠an move¬≠ment ‚Äî and the one whose imprac¬≠ti¬≠cal¬≠i¬≠ty came soon¬≠est to light.


	

Before he became Brazil‚Äôs most famous archi¬≠tect, Oscar Niemey¬≠er sub¬≠scribed to the prin¬≠ci¬≠ples of the Inter¬≠na¬≠tion¬≠al Style. But then, as an acolyte of Le Cor¬≠busier, he could hard¬≠ly have done oth¬≠er¬≠wise. When the great man came to Rio de Janeiro in 1936 to design the new Min¬≠istry of Edu¬≠ca¬≠tion and Health, Niemey¬≠er was hired to work on the project.
The expe¬≠ri¬≠ence seems to have done its part to con¬≠vince him that the Inter¬≠na¬≠tion¬≠al Style was¬≠n‚Äôt as inter¬≠na¬≠tion¬≠al as all that, and fur¬≠ther¬≠more, that its rigid dic¬≠tates would have to be bent to suit his home¬≠land. This bend¬≠ing would, in a sense, be lit¬≠er¬≠al: like Frank Gehry or Zaha Hadid after him, Niemey¬≠er devot¬≠ed his archi¬≠tec¬≠ture to the pur¬≠suit of the curve, inspired by exam¬≠ples seen in every¬≠thing from the moun¬≠tains of Brazil‚Äôs land¬≠scape to the bod¬≠ies of its women.


	

In 1956, the new¬≠ly elect¬≠ed pres¬≠i¬≠dent¬ÝJusceli¬≠no Kubitschek imme¬≠di¬≠ate¬≠ly real¬≠ized the plan, writ¬≠ten into the coun¬≠try‚Äôs con¬≠sti¬≠tu¬≠tion long before, of build¬≠ing a new cen¬≠tral city to relieve Rio of its sta¬≠tus as the cap¬≠i¬≠tal. Chris¬≠tened Bras√≠lia, it was to be con¬≠struct¬≠ed on a vast, emp¬≠ty plateau entire¬≠ly along ratio¬≠nal, mod¬≠ernist guide¬≠lines, with defined dis¬≠tricts orga¬≠nized along a cru¬≠ci¬≠form city plan often likened to a bird or an air¬≠plane and mon¬≠u¬≠men¬≠tal struc¬≠tures meant to project a for¬≠ward-look¬≠ing image. Niemey¬≠er was select¬≠ed to design those struc¬≠tures, which imme¬≠di¬≠ate¬≠ly became ele¬≠ments of the city‚Äôs visu¬≠al sig¬≠na¬≠ture upon its inau¬≠gu¬≠ra¬≠tion in 1960: ever since, sel¬≠dom has a pho¬≠to¬≠graph failed to include the twin tow¬≠ers and domes of his Nation¬≠al Con¬≠gress or the¬ÝSpace-Age¬Ýcrown of thorns atop his Cathe¬≠dral of Bras√≠lia.


	

The both admin¬≠is¬≠tra¬≠tive and oth¬≠er¬≠world¬≠ly form of cen¬≠tral Bras√≠lia remains allur¬≠ing, though the city itself began draw¬≠ing crit¬≠i¬≠cism even before its com¬≠ple¬≠tion. ‚ÄúThis is what you get when per¬≠fect¬≠ly decent, intel¬≠li¬≠gent and tal¬≠ent¬≠ed men start think¬≠ing in terms of space, rather than place, and about sin¬≠gle rather than mul¬≠ti¬≠ple mean¬≠ings,‚Äù declared a frown¬≠ing Robert Hugh¬≠es in his 1980¬ÝTV series The Shock of the New. ‚ÄúIt‚Äôs what you get when you design for polit¬≠i¬≠cal aspi¬≠ra¬≠tions and not real human needs. You get miles of jer¬≠ry-built pla¬≠ton¬≠ic nowhere infest¬≠ed with Volk¬≠swa¬≠gens.‚Äù¬ÝIndeed, the dom¬≠i¬≠na¬≠tion of car infra¬≠struc¬≠ture and strict sep¬≠a¬≠ra¬≠tion of func¬≠tions hard¬≠ly proved con¬≠ducive to the spon¬≠ta¬≠neous,¬Ýcon¬≠vivial aspects of Brazil¬≠ian life. But res¬≠i¬≠dents and vis¬≠i¬≠tors alike tend to report that Bras√≠li¬≠a‚Äôs urban design has been improved as its pop¬≠u¬≠la¬≠tion has grown, and mas¬≠sive¬≠ly, with com¬≠men¬≠su¬≠rate improve¬≠ments to its qual¬≠i¬≠ty of life over the decades. It may not inspire many bossa nova songs, but the cap¬≠i¬≠tal nev¬≠er¬≠the¬≠less reflects a gen¬≠uine facet of what Brazil is ‚Äî and what it once dreamed of becom¬≠ing.
Relat¬≠ed Con¬≠tent:
A is for Archi¬≠tec¬≠ture: 1960 Doc¬≠u¬≠men¬≠tary on Why We Build, from the Ancient Greeks to Mod¬≠ern Times
Leonar¬≠do da Vin¬≠ci Designs the Ide¬≠al City: See 3D Mod¬≠els of His Rad¬≠i¬≠cal Design
Why Dutch & Japan¬≠ese Cities Are Insane¬≠ly Well Designed (and Amer¬≠i¬≠can Cities Are Ter¬≠ri¬≠bly Designed)
Frank Lloyd Wright Designs an Urban Utopia: See His Hand-Drawn Sketch¬≠es of Broad¬≠acre City (1932)
The World Accord¬≠ing to Le Cor¬≠busier: An Ani¬≠mat¬≠ed Intro¬≠duc¬≠tion to the Most Mod¬≠ern of All Archi¬≠tects
Take a Vir¬≠tu¬≠al Tour of Brazil‚Äôs Nation¬≠al Muse¬≠um & Its Arti¬≠facts: Google Dig¬≠i¬≠tized the Museum‚Äôs Col¬≠lec¬≠tion Before the Fate¬≠ful Fire
Based in Seoul,¬ÝCol¬≠in¬ÝMarshall¬Ýwrites and broad¬≠casts on cities, lan¬≠guage, and cul¬≠ture. He‚Äôs the author of the newslet¬≠ter¬ÝBooks on Cities¬Ýas well as the books¬ÝÌïúÍµ≠ ÏöîÏïΩ Í∏àÏßÄ¬Ý(No Sum¬≠ma¬≠riz¬≠ing Korea) and¬ÝKore¬≠an Newtro.¬ÝFol¬≠low him on the social net¬≠work for¬≠mer¬≠ly known as Twit¬≠ter at¬Ý@colinmarshall.

title: Discover the Copiale Cipher: The Mysterious 18th-Century Book That Took 260 Years to Decode
description:
in Architecture, Books, History |   	April 27th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

In the world of cryp¬≠tog¬≠ra¬≠phy, sub¬≠sti¬≠tu¬≠tion ciphers are child‚Äô
body:
in Architecture, Books, History |   	April 27th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

In the world of cryp¬≠tog¬≠ra¬≠phy, sub¬≠sti¬≠tu¬≠tion ciphers are child‚Äôs play. Indeed, we may remem¬≠ber lit¬≠er¬≠al¬≠ly play¬≠ing with them as chil¬≠dren, writ¬≠ing secret mes¬≠sages to our friends by replac¬≠ing all the let¬≠ters with num¬≠bers, say, or shift¬≠ing them one or two places over in alpha¬≠bet¬≠i¬≠cal order. Crack¬≠ing such codes was a triv¬≠ial mat¬≠ter even before the com¬≠put¬≠er age, but cer¬≠tain sim¬≠ple vari¬≠a¬≠tions could make them more robust. Take the doc¬≠u¬≠ment known as the Copi¬≠ale cipher (down¬≠load¬≠able as a two-part PDF), a 105-page bound man¬≠u¬≠script that stayed unde¬≠ci¬≠pher¬≠able for more than 260 years. Its mys¬≠tery final¬≠ly yield¬≠ed to the efforts of Uni¬≠ver¬≠si¬≠ty of South¬≠ern Cal¬≠i¬≠for¬≠nia com¬≠put¬≠er sci¬≠en¬≠tist Kevin Knight and Upp¬≠sala Uni¬≠ver¬≠si¬≠ty lin¬≠guists Bea¬≠ta Megye¬≠si and Chris¬≠tiane Schae¬≠fer only in the ear¬≠ly twen¬≠ty-tens.
As Tom¬≠mie Trelawny tells the sto¬≠ry of the Copi¬≠ale cipher in the Hochela¬≠ga video above, the man¬≠u¬≠script, which was orig¬≠i¬≠nal¬≠ly thought to date between 1760 and 1780, first had to be con¬≠vert¬≠ed into machine-read¬≠able code. The tex¬≠t‚Äôs use of 88 unique sym¬≠bols, one of them shaped like an eye, neces¬≠si¬≠tat¬≠ed com¬≠ing up with names for all of them apart from the Roman let¬≠ters, which had no par¬≠tic¬≠u¬≠lar mean¬≠ing in iso¬≠la¬≠tion.
When anoth¬≠er scan searched for repeat¬≠ed let¬≠ter com¬≠bi¬≠na¬≠tions, its results shed light on prob¬≠a¬≠ble sim¬≠i¬≠lar¬≠i¬≠ties with the Ger¬≠man lan¬≠guage. This made sense, since the book was found in Ger¬≠many in the first place. Could mul¬≠ti¬≠ple sym¬≠bols in this strange cipher have been sub¬≠sti¬≠tut¬≠ed for sin¬≠gle Ger¬≠man let¬≠ters? Could the code be, in cryp¬≠to¬≠graph¬≠ic terms, a homo¬≠phon¬≠ic cipher?

Approach¬≠ing the text under that hypoth¬≠e¬≠sis revealed mean¬≠ings sug¬≠gest¬≠ing, tan¬≠ta¬≠liz¬≠ing¬≠ly, that it had been writ¬≠ten by a secret soci¬≠ety. It even describes an ini¬≠ti¬≠a¬≠tion rit¬≠u¬≠al in which the inductee must first ‚Äúread‚Äù a blank piece of paper, then try again with eye¬≠glass¬≠es, then again after wash¬≠ing his eyes, and then, final¬≠ly, under¬≠go a sym¬≠bol¬≠ic ‚Äúoper¬≠a¬≠tion‚Äù involv¬≠ing the pluck¬≠ing of a sin¬≠gle eye¬≠brow. This soci¬≠ety, the Oculists, turns out to have been com¬≠posed entire¬≠ly of oph¬≠thal¬≠mol¬≠o¬≠gists meet¬≠ing in the sev¬≠en¬≠teen-for¬≠ties. That they did so covert¬≠ly may owe to their hav¬≠ing been Freema¬≠sons, whose rites had recent¬≠ly been banned by Pope Clement XII. The Copi¬≠ale cipher sug¬≠gests that Oculists appear to have had no aims more sin¬≠is¬≠ter than the pur¬≠suit of knowl¬≠edge ‚Äî not that, for most of us today, the notion of eigh¬≠teenth-cen¬≠tu¬≠ry eye surgery isn‚Äôt ter¬≠ri¬≠fy¬≠ing enough.


	

Relat¬≠ed con¬≠tent:
Explore a Dig¬≠i¬≠tized Edi¬≠tion of the Voyn¬≠ich Man¬≠u¬≠script, ‚Äúthe World‚Äôs Most Mys¬≠te¬≠ri¬≠ous Book‚Äù
The Enig¬≠ma Machine: How Alan Tur¬≠ing Helped Break the Unbreak¬≠able Nazi Code
Three Ama¬≠teur Cryp¬≠tog¬≠ra¬≠phers Final¬≠ly Decrypt¬≠ed the Zodi¬≠ac Killer‚Äôs Let¬≠ters: A Look Inside How They Solved a Half Cen¬≠tu¬≠ry-Old Mys¬≠tery
The Rohonc Codex: Hungary‚Äôs Mys¬≠te¬≠ri¬≠ous Man¬≠u¬≠script That No One Can Read
The Codex Seraphini¬≠anus: How Ital¬≠ian Artist Lui¬≠gi Ser¬≠afi¬≠ni Came to Write & Illus¬≠trate ‚Äúthe Strangest Book Ever Pub¬≠lished‚Äù (1981)
Can You Crack the Uncrack¬≠able Code in Kryp¬≠tos, the CIA‚Äôs Work of Pub¬≠lic Art?
Based in Seoul,¬ÝCol¬≠in¬ÝMarshall¬Ýwrites and broad¬≠casts on cities, lan¬≠guage, and cul¬≠ture. He‚Äôs the author of the newslet¬≠ter¬ÝBooks on Cities¬Ýas well as the books¬ÝÌïúÍµ≠ ÏöîÏïΩ Í∏àÏßÄ¬Ý(No Sum¬≠ma¬≠riz¬≠ing Korea) and¬ÝKore¬≠an Newtro.¬ÝFol¬≠low him on the social net¬≠work for¬≠mer¬≠ly known as Twit¬≠ter at¬Ý@colinmarshall.

title: Giant Buddha Lands in New York
description:
Daily Newsletter
                A nude performance, a photography fair turns to craft, and a review of Alice Tippit's erotics
body:
Daily Newsletter
                A nude performance, a photography fair turns to craft, and a review of Alice Tippit's erotics
            
                
                                
                            
                
            
        
            Last week, artist Xandra Ibarra, nearly nude, strode through the contemporary galleries of the Museum of Fine Arts, laughing all the while. It was part of a piece appropriately named ‚ÄúNude Laughing,‚Äù which she‚Äôs staged at various art venues for the last dozen years. Read Staff Reporter Rhea Nayyar‚Äôs report on the performance, which rattled museum visitors, scandalized Instagram commentators, and shook Boston to its puritanical core.¬ÝSpeaking of artworks that challenge the limits of the art world, tune in this Wednesday, 3‚Äì4pm (ET), for a conversation between Editor-at-Large Hrag Vartanian and Indigenous weaver and MacArthur fellow Jeremy Frey. The two will discuss the process of making baskets that blend craft, design, and contemporary art, from harvesting materials to museum installation. Become a member here ‚Äî¬Ýwe‚Äôd love to have you. ‚ÄîLisa Yin Zhang, associate editor
Opening night at the Photography Show (photo Isa Farfan/Hyperallergic)Refreshing Turn to Craft at AIPAD‚Äôs Photography ShowIn a year of AI image corruption, this year‚Äôs fair, focused largely on Latin American and Latine artists, feels especially hopeful. | Isa Farfan
                    
                    
                    
                        
                            Rising Up: Rocky and the Making of MonumentsFeaturing works from antiquity to today, the exhibition at the Philadelphia Museum of Art explores the Rocky statue and its impact on the city‚Äôs culture, community, and public art.
                        
                        
                            Learn more
                        
                    
            NewsA mid-performance shot of Xandra Ibarra‚Äôs ‚ÄúNude Laughing‚Äù (2014), (photo Tim Correira, courtesy MFA Boston)Artist Xandra Ibarra‚Äôs nude performance at MFA Boston sparks conversations about consent, viewer etiquette, art history, and the human body.The High Line's new 27-foot-tall Buddha is a resurrection of¬Ýa critical piece of destroyed cultural heritage ‚Äî the Bamiyan Buddhas.From Our CriticsAlice Tippit, ‚ÄúShare‚Äù (2020), oil on canvas (courtesy Friendly Frames)Alice Tippit‚Äôs Mischievous EroticsThe artist‚Äôs mixed messages suggest a deep skepticism about the ability of language to adequately express human experience. | Lori WaxmanMark Milroy Sees, Remembers, and Imagines at OnceHis deliberate gaze, which mixes personal memory and art historical insight, makes his work special. | John YauJan Staller Photographs the Nuts and Bolts of Manhattan‚Äôs Urban SymphonyHis new photography book transforms the city‚Äôs endless construction sites into otherworldly visions. | Mellissa Holbrook Pierson
                    
                    
                    
                        
                            The Marsden Hartley Legacy Project Launches OnlineThe first comprehensive catalogue of artworks by acclaimed modernist painter Marsden Hartley is now freely available on the internet.
                        
                        
                            Learn more
                        
                    
            Photo of Hadieh Afshani's studioA View From the Easel: Hadieh Afshani‚ÄúA certain level of distraction can help me see the work more clearly.‚ÄùRemembering James Hayward, LA‚Äôs Adored Cowboy PainterA legendary artist, Jimmy was equally known for his magnetic personality, sense of ribald humor, and inspired storytelling.Holly Wong on Randa Jumean‚Äôs ‚ÄúImage of Family Torn by ICE Wins World Press Photo of the Year‚ÄùGosh "Separated by ICE" says it all. I am glad this important work is being elevated. It couldn't be more important.From the ArchiveEsther Pressoir Is the Coolest Artist You‚Äôve Never Heard OfIn 1927, Pressoir carried 30 pounds of art-making supplies on a bike ride from France to Italy. It was just the beginning of an inimitable artistic journey. | Bridget Quinn
                  
                      
                        Daily Newsletter
                      
                      
                        Newsletter

title: Artists Sell More Than $1 M. in Art at Sotheby‚Äôs in Support of a Debt-Free Yale MFA Program
description: Artists ranging from Mickalene Thomas to Tammy Nguyen are banding together to sell more than $1 million in art at Sotheby‚Äôs next month, with all the
body:
Artists ranging from Mickalene Thomas to Tammy Nguyen are banding together to sell more than $1 million in art at Sotheby‚Äôs next month, with all the funds going toward Yale University‚Äôs MFA art program, among the most esteemed ones of its kind in the country. All of the works will appear in a contemporary art day sale during the marquee auctions. 

	The most expensive piece of the bunch, a 2005 Richard Prince photograph of a clothed, adult Brooke Shields from his ‚ÄúSpiritual America‚Äù series, has a $500,000‚Äì$700,000 estimate. That may be a fraction of Prince‚Äôs $9.7 million auction record, but if the photograph sells at the low end of its estimate, the intake could still be enough to pay the tuition for 10 MFA students for one year. The Prince piece comes to auction from a committee that includes Yvonne Force and Leo Villareal, Iwan and Manuela Wirth, Esther Kim, Carol LeWitt, Linda Macklowe, Yana Peel, Komal Shah, and Thomas.
	

	
		Related Articles
	


	Speaking by phone with ARTnews, Kymberly Pinder, the dean of the Yale School of Art, said she was hoping to raise somewhere between $1 million and $2 million through the Sotheby‚Äôs sale. ‚ÄúIt will definitely increase how much I can give to students who are in need,‚Äù she said.

	When she was hired in June 2021, Pinder set out to find a way to wipe the cost of tuition for Yale‚Äôs MFA art program, as the university‚Äôs graduate music program would do that month through a $150 million donation from David Geffen. ‚ÄúThe word ‚Äòethical‚Äô came into my head,‚Äù Pinder said. ‚ÄúI was like, ‚ÄòThis is just not ethical.‚Äô It should be an ethical MFA. People are giving so much time and resources toward something for two years that it shouldn‚Äôt be a punishment when they leave. They shouldn‚Äôt be saddled with so much debt.‚Äù

	Annual tuition for the Yale School of Art currently stands at just under $50,200‚Äîa tough barrier to clear for most aspiring artists looking to attend a program that still acts as a feeder for many New York institutions and galleries. In order to pay that price, artists often take out large loans. This led artist Josh Kline to remark, in a recent essay for October, that the Yale School of Art is ‚Äúpowered by student debt,‚Äù just like many other university-run programs of its kind.
	

	Pinder said she had already raised $11 million toward meeting her goal for making tuition for Yale‚Äôs graduate art program free, though she declined to state an exact number, saying that the economic and political climate had shifted it since she began the initiative. Still, she said, ‚Äúwe‚Äôre pretty far from making the school completely tuition-free.‚Äù

	The works headed to Sotheby‚Äôs include an array of works by Yale alumni. Some of those alumni are historical‚Äîfor example, Walker Evans and Josef Albers. Others, however, are more recent: Dominic Chambers is sending a painting of the artist Shikeith with a $40,000‚Äì$60,000 price tag, while Thomas has a glittery painting of the dancer Josephine Baker pegged at $250,000‚Äì$300,000. Also included are works by Elaine Reichek, Barkley L. Hendricks, and Howardena Pindell.


			
			
			
	
			
					Do Ho Suh, Staircases, 2019.
									Courtesy Sotheby‚Äôs
	



	Do Ho Suh, a Yale MFA alum who will have a Tate Modern survey this summer, donated $200,000‚Äì$300,000 work on paper composed of thread arranged to form staircases. Suh described consigning the piece as a natural extension of his longtime support of the Yale School of Art. ‚ÄúI started to make small donations as soon as I graduated, because I wanted to give something back to the school, even though I was struggling young artist,‚Äù he said in a phone conversation.
	

	The Korean-born artist said he loved his time at Yale, but because he was born abroad, he struggled. ‚ÄúBecause of all the expenses, it was very difficult for me‚ÄîI couldn‚Äôt have any financial aid because the grants were only for Americans,‚Äù he said. ‚ÄúSo, it was really challenging. And my understanding is it‚Äôs getting harder. That‚Äôs one of the reasons I decided to donate this work, to support young talent.‚Äù

schema.headline: Artists Sell More Than $1 M. in Art at Sotheby‚Äôs in Support of a Debt-Free Yale MFA Program

title: Israel Addresses Venice Biennale Jury‚Äôs ‚ÄòBoycott‚Äô of Pavilion: ‚ÄòA Contamination of the Art World‚Äô
description: Israel has responded to a statement released by the Venice Biennale jury, in which its five curators said last week that they would not consider any
body:
Israel has responded to a statement released by the Venice Biennale jury, in which its five curators said last week that they would not consider any pavilions by countries who are charged with crimes against humanity by the International Criminal Court.

	Calling that move a ‚Äúboycott,‚Äù Israel‚Äôs foreign ministry denounced the jury in a statement on X this morning that accused the group of aiding in ‚Äúa contamination of the art world.‚Äù

	‚ÄúThe political jury has transformed the Biennale from an open artistic space of free, boundless ideas into a spectacle of false, anti-Israeli political indoctrination,‚Äù Israel‚Äôs foreign ministry said in its statement.
	

	
		Related Articles
	


	Belu-Simion Fainaru, the artist representing Israel, also denounced the jury in a statement issued last week.

	‚ÄúThe Jury statement creates a hostile and degrading environment. And puts Unequal condition imposed solely on the Israeli participant,‚Äù Fainaru wrote.¬Ý‚ÄúIn this way the jury ¬Ýexceeded its mandate.¬ÝI must mention that other states with serious violations are not excluded. This statement is the violation of essential equality condition based on legally unstable and arbitrary basis.‚Äù

	Previously, the jury labeled its decision not to consider nations charged by the ICC a ‚Äúdefense of human rights.‚Äù 

	Russian President Vladimir Putin, much like Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu, is facing an arrest warrant from the ICC, and so Russia will not be considered by the jury, either. Putin and Netanyahu were condemned for their nations‚Äô invasions of Ukraine and Gaza, respectively.

	Despite controversy over both pavilions, the Biennale‚Äôs organizers have claimed it cannot kick either nation out of the exhibition, saying that the show can host any country recognized as a state within Italy.

	The Biennale, which selected the jury as a result of the untimely passing of Koyo Kouoh, the curator of the main exhibition, has sought to distance itself from the jury, saying last week, ‚ÄúWith regard to the statement issued today by the Jurors, this is a position that the members have chosen to anticipate and make public. It represents a natural expression of the freedom and autonomy which La Biennale guarantees.‚Äù

schema.headline: Israel Addresses Venice Biennale Jury‚Äôs ‚ÄòBoycott‚Äô of Pavilion: ‚ÄòA Contamination of the Art World‚Äô

title: ‚ÄúPrimary Structures,‚Äù Turns 60
description: On April 28, 1966, The New York Times ran a review of the Jewish Museum‚Äôs ‚ÄúPrimary Structures‚Äù exhibition by Hilton Kramer, a conservative critic no
body:
On April 28, 1966, The New York Times ran a review of the Jewish Museum‚Äôs ‚ÄúPrimary Structures‚Äù exhibition by Hilton Kramer, a conservative critic notorious for his disdain for contemporary art. That contempt was amply displayed in his views on the show: ‚ÄúHere are 42 American and British artists,‚Äù Kramer observed, ‚Äúwho care nothing for the personal touch, the subjective inflection, the private vocabulary‚Äîthe whole panoply of individual expressive devices that have yielded modern painting and sculpture some of their most glorious achievements.‚Äù
	

	Instead, he continued, their work demonstrated ‚Äúan air of extreme detachment, of theory condescending to realize itself in the concrete specifications of a particular object.‚Äù Still, he understood that the exhibition, organized by legendary curator Kynaston McShine, represented the ‚Äúfirst comprehensive glimpse of a style that promises‚Äîor perhaps one should say threatens‚Äîto become our period style. We are going to see a good many more of these ‚ÄòPrimary Structures‚Äô before the nineteen-sixties have come to an end.‚Äù

	
		Related Articles
	


	And so it came to pass, as ‚ÄúPrimary Structures‚Äù offered the ur-survey of what would be called Minimalism‚Äîa name eventually hated by many of the artists to whom it was applied. Minimalism impacted not only art, but also architecture, fashion, design, and just about everything else. The term became a veritable synonym for a thing or idea that was reductive in nature, and it continues to shape culture today. With the 60th anniversary of ‚ÄúPrimary Structures‚Äù approaching, it‚Äôs worth looking back at the show and the mid-century art world that birthed it.

	That world was much smaller than it is now, and the belief‚Äîsince challenged by recent shows like Whitechapel Gallery‚Äôs ‚ÄúAdventures of the Black Square: Abstract Art and Society 1915‚Äì2015‚Äù (2015), Tate‚Äôs ‚ÄúThe World Goes Pop‚Äù (2015‚Äì16), the Met‚Äôs ‚ÄúSurrealism Beyond Borders‚Äù (2021‚Äì22), and even the Jewish Museum‚Äôs own reworking of ‚ÄúPrimary Structures‚Äù in 2014‚Äîstill held that innovative art, generally suffixed with ‚Äú-ism,‚Äù was conditioned on a particular place (Paris during the 19th century, New York during the 20th). Likewise, the concomitant avant-garde narrative that art history was driven by a succession of cutting-edge styles, with each supplanting its predecessor, was still honored. Since many interpreted this arc to mean a progressive shedding of detail, or evidence of the artist‚Äôs hand, Minimalism seemed like a logical culmination of modernism.
	

	‚ÄúPrimary Structures‚Äù included a roster of then little-known names who, per Kramer‚Äôs prediction, became a who‚Äôs who of 1960s art: the Americans Carl Andre, Dan Flavin, Donald Judd, Sol LeWitt, Walter De Maria, Robert Morris, Anne Truitt, John McCracken, Larry Bell, Robert Smithson, and Judy Chicago; and British participants including Philip King, Michael Bolus, and David Annesley.

	As the organizer of the show, however, McShine never used the word Minimalism, which, like so many labels in art history, was coined after the fact as a one-size-fits-all descriptor for a range of differing approaches. In fact, he studiously avoided categorizing the work, letting it speak for itself.

	One thing the artists of ‚ÄúPrimary Structures‚Äù did share was an historical debt to the early-Modern and postwar practitioners of geometric abstraction. Kramer himself noted connections to Kazimir Malevich and Piet Mondrian, but he might have added the AbEx-adjacent likes of Ad Reinhardt and Barnett Newman.

	Still, these earlier artists differed significantly from the participants in ‚ÄúPrimary Structures‚Äù in their intentions. Malevich and Mondrian saw painting as a spiritual platform, purged of all but its most essential elements. Newman‚Äôs vast color fields were meant to overwhelm viewers with a sublime sensory overload. Reinhardt‚Äôs ‚ÄúBlack Paintings‚Äù were probably closest to Minimalism, though in fact he viewed them as a form of liberation from the yoke of art history.
	

	By contrast, many of the artists in ‚ÄúPrimary Structures‚Äù pushed formalism to a state of totalizing neutrality, most conspicuously in the concept of the ‚Äúspecific object‚Äù developed by Donald Judd in his 1964 essay of the same name. Basically, Judd rejected any kind of referential role for a work of art, insisting that its ‚Äúmeaning‚Äù lay strictly in its autonomous occupation of three-dimensional space‚Äîa thing to be apprehended all at once rather than as a composition of parts. This meant an absolute renunciation of painting for being illusionistic, no matter the type. ‚ÄúActual space is intrinsically more powerful and specific than paint on a flat surface,‚Äù he wrote, putting paid to an entire medium, though that hardly stopped anyone from wanting to pursue it.

	Judd had his work fabricated by others, something that particularly rankled Kramer. But this methodology spoke to a uniquely American, can-do sensibility based on the hardware store and factory floor (Judd‚Äôs work was often serial in nature), as well as on an absenting of history.

	McShine wasn‚Äôt nearly as militant in his thinking. He included artists like the Brit Anthony Caro, whose sculptures constituted a form of pictorialism made concrete, an expression derived from David Smith, a sculptor whose formative work was influenced by Picasso. Along with Judd precursor Tony Smith, McShine positioned Caro as a pillar of the exhibition and its themes.
	

	‚ÄúPrimary Structures‚Äù was immediately recognized for its break with convention, especially in the way it suggested a shift in the artist‚Äôs role from maker to designer. Indeed, Judd wasn‚Äôt alone in having his work fabricated by others, and it remains a standard procedure in contemporary art.

	For all of Judd‚Äôs talk about the deficiencies of painting, though, much of the work in the show‚Äîincluding his own‚Äîpacked a graphic punch that was usually native to 2D imagery. Many of the artists on view employed a ‚Äúfree-wheeling use of color,‚Äù as Kramer put it, as well as planar shapes that would feel at home on canvas.

	Ultimately ‚ÄúPrimary Structures‚Äù was inseparable from its venue, as the Jewish Museum was one of the prime movers of contemporary art at a time when its program included the first retrospectives of Robert Rauschenberg and Jasper Johns. Though this phase in the museum‚Äôs existence was brief, it spoke to an era when ideas in art still counted, no matter the pushback. Even Kramer had to concede that the show ‚Äúconclusively demonstrates the flourishing existence of an entirely new sculptural esthetics.‚Äù

title: LA‚Äôs The Box Gallery to Close After 19 Years
description:
The Box in Los Angeles announced this weekend that it would close after 19 years in business.

	Its final show was a two-venue collaboration with Pa
body:
The Box in Los Angeles announced this weekend that it would close after 19 years in business.

	Its final show was a two-venue collaboration with Parker Gallery for the late California artist Wally Hedrick, which ran through April 4. The gallery said they would mark the closure with a fashion show for Johanna Went, done in collaboration with artist and playwright Asher Hartman on June 6.

	‚ÄúWhile this decision has been brewing for some time, it has landed with urgency, shaped by a set of circumstances that made continuing impossible,‚Äù gallery founder Mara McCarthy wrote in a statement sent to its email listserv and posted on Instagram. ‚ÄúIt feels right to end this way, with¬Ýthe¬Ýkind of work we always existed to support: radical, enlightening, and not easily contained by¬Ýthe¬Ýcommercial marketplace.‚Äù
	

	
		Related Articles
	


	Located at 805 Traction Avenue in downtown LA, The Box opened in June 2007 with a multichannel video installation by Spandau Parks. In her statement, McCarthy described the impetus behind creating The Box as a collaboration with her father, the iconic LA-based artist Paul McCarthy. Though active since the 1970s, Paul did not reach wider acclaim until the 1990s with his inclusion in the landmark 1992 exhibition ‚ÄúHelter Skelter‚Äù at the Museum of Contemporary Art Los Angeles.

	Calling it an ‚Äúartist-formed space‚Äù since its beginning, Mara said The Box ‚Äúwas shaped as a response to his market success, which arrived late in his career,‚Äù with the aim of uplifting the work of Paul‚Äôs peers, like Barbara T. Smith and Simone Forti, who had not yet achieved success. ‚ÄúThe¬ÝBox¬Ýformed to help fill this void,‚Äù Mara wrote.

	The gallery would dedicate its third exhibition to Smith, presenting two monumental works by the artist made in the mid-1960s and early ‚Äô70s. Subsequent exhibitions would be for Forti, Hedrick, Paul McCarthy, John Altoon, Naotaka Hiro Stan Vanderbeek, Judith Bernstein, and Leigh Ledare. Through this exhibition program, which shed a light on a generation of LA artists, The Box quickly established itself as a venturesome gallery and one of the most important in the city.

	‚ÄúWhile our program has often resembled what a nonprofit art space might embrace, we made a deliberate choice to engage¬Ýthe¬Ýmarketplace as a for-profit gallery, with¬Ýthe¬Ýcapacity to actively shape a presence for experimental artists whose work had otherwise gone unrecognized,‚Äù Mara wrote in her statement. ‚ÄúThe¬Ýtruth is that our program has been sustained in large part by¬Ýthe¬Ýgenerous support of McCarthy Studios, and by my mother and father‚Äôs steadfast vision to seed¬Ýthe¬Ýtype of arts community and economy¬Ýthey hoped to see bloom. But¬Ýthe¬Ýmarket has not always readily embraced¬Ýthe¬Ýwork we champion.‚Äù ¬Ý
	

	She attributed the decision to close in part to the changing economics around support for her father‚Äôs work, as well as the fact that every member of her family lost their homes in the Eaton fire that ravaged Altadena and other parts of the San Gabriel Vallery in January 2025.

	The Box is the latest in a wave of gallery closures that have affected several commercial enterprises around the world, but particularly in Los Angeles. Earlier this week, Marian Goodman Gallery announced it would close its LA space, while last year, Tanya Bonakdar announced it would close its LA outpost. LA Louver, which had been in business for 50 years, closed its doors in September, shifting to private dealing, while the summer also saw dealer Tim Blum close his LA gallery after 30 years.

	On Instagram, the post had garnered nearly 2,000 likes and over 300 comments by Saturday morning. Clara Kim, the chief curator of MOCA LA, wrote, ‚ÄúSending much love and respect, Mara. Thanks to you, Robert and The Box for an incredible run of thoughtful, provocative shows, championing artists and art scenes from the past and present. We have all been better for it. What a major loss for LA.‚Äù

	Stuart Comer, chief curator of media and performance at New York‚Äôs Museum of Modern Art, added, ‚ÄúMara, you moved the needle in ways very few can, have, or will again. Sending so much love to you and your family. ‚ù§Ô∏è‚ù§Ô∏è‚ù§Ô∏è‚Äù
	

	In her statement, Mara McCarthy added, ‚ÄúAs a woman, a curator, and someone who long ago understood¬Ýthe¬Ýpower of art to alter¬Ýthe¬Ýcourse of a life, I will not stop being present in this world, and I will not stop advocating for¬Ýthe¬Ýartists I believe in. It has been among my deepest joys to exhibit and foster¬Ýtheir work. Over¬Ýthe¬Ýyears I have come to understand that supporting artists takes many forms, and exhibitions are only one of¬Ýthem. What many artists need most is conversation, presence, and genuine engagement, and that will not stop for me.‚Äù

schema.headline: LA‚Äôs The Box Gallery to Close After 19 Years
